pattern: Once everyone is paired up, a friend's ranked wishlist plus their partner's wishlist is all you need -- turn each wishlist into a lookup of "how much do I prefer this person" and unhappiness becomes a simple mutual-preference check between every pair of partners.
hints: For every person, convert their preference list into a lookup that tells you how much they prefer any other given person -- a smaller number means a stronger preference.

A friend x is unhappy if there is some other pair (u, v) where x prefers u to their own partner, and u also prefers x to their own partner v -- so you only need to compare x against people x ranks above their current partner.

Because you already have O(1) preference lookups, you can stop scanning x's wishlist as soon as you reach their actual partner: everyone before that point is a candidate u, and for each you just check u's lookup for x versus u's own partner.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two unhappy friends among four
statement example, smallest case, everyone happy
statement example, all four friends unhappy
n=2 with reversed pair order in the pairs list
everyone's top choice is already their partner, so nobody is unhappy despite full-length wishlists
a chain where x prefers u over their partner but u does not prefer x back, so x stays happy
pairs listed with the higher index first, must not assume pair ordering
eight friends, symmetric mutual crush spanning two different pairs

stress.description: random even n with shuffled preference lists and randomized pairing
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size if size % 2 == 0 else size - 1
    n = max(n, 2)
    prefs = []
    for i in range(n):
        others = [j for j in range(n) if j != i]
        rng.shuffle(others)
        prefs.append(others)
    people = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(people)
    pairs = [[people[k], people[k + 1]] for k in range(0, n, 2)]
    return [n, prefs, pairs]
